events.event_label: Saturated fatty acids detected in rice seedlings
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s001: A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1. PMC9183074.p017.s002: Eight saturated fatty acids, namely lauric acid (C12:0), myristic acid (C14:0), pentadecanoic acid (C15:0), palmitic acid (C16:0), stearic acid (C18:0), arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0), were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars in both control and HT conditions. PMC9183074.p017.s003: Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p017.s004: Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments.

events.event_label: HT stress reduces membrane stability and increases EL
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s001: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A). PMC9183074.p012.s002: When subjected to HT stress, cellular membranes were damaged, and all rice genotypes showed significantly higher EL. PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22.

events.event_label: HT decreases PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction. PMC9183074.p024.s005: The chloroplast photosynthetic function is supposed to be the most HT-sensitive cellular function, and PSII is one of the most sensitive to HT. PMC9183074.p024.s006: In this study, PSII efficiency was determined by measuring the maximum quantum yield in the dark-adapted stage (Fv/Fm), which decreased significantly after exposure to HT in CN1, KDML105 and IR64 but remained unchanged in the heat-tolerant genotype, N22 (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p024.s007: Heat-induced reduction in Fv/Fm resulted from an increase in minimum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (F0) which indicated the inhibition of electron transport and a reduction in maximum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (Fm) that is caused by a dissociation of peripheral antenna complex of PSII from its core complex.

events.event_label: HT stress alters fatty acid abundance (increase capric, decrease palmitic and stearic)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s003: Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p017.s004: Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments. PMC9183074.p017.s005: Palmitic acid (C16:0) was found in the greatest amount under both control and HT conditions. PMC9183074.p017.s006: Palmitic acid and stearic acid markedly decreased with HT in all cultivars. PMC9183074.p017.s007: Three saturated fatty acids including arachidic acid, behenic acid, and lignoceric acid in N22, CN1 and IR64 were significantly increased after HT treatment, but in KDML105, the contents of these acids declined significantly.

events.event_label: Long-chain fatty acids promote cuticular wax synthesis, contributing to heat tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions. PMC9183074.p002.s008: Dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to cuticular wax synthesis which provides protective roles for heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p002.s009: Thus, the reduction in unsaturated fatty acid composition of the thylakoid membrane and dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to high photosynthetic performance and an enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax which further provided additional protective roles for heat tolerance ability in rice.

events.event_label: HT disrupts cell membranes and induces leaf senescence
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p005.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive site affected by environmental stimulation, including HT. PMC9183074.p005.s002: HT has been reported to be associated with disrupting cell membranes, breaking down cell compartmentalization, leading to solute leakage and affecting cellular functions that cause physiological injuries and leaf senescence. PMC9183074.p005.s003: The disruption of a cellular membrane by HT is related to the increase in membrane lipid peroxidation, which occurs when double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids are attacked by ROS.

events.event_label: High temperature increases saturated fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s018: HT led to changes in lipid membrane structure and membrane fluidity relating to changes in lipid composition and saturation level. PMC9183074.p007.s019: Increased lipid saturation level was reported in creeping bentgrass exposed to high soil temperature (35 °C) or high ambient temperature (35 °C). PMC9183074.p007.s020: In tall fescue turfgrass, increased lipid unsaturation was reported when exposed to HT (40/35 °C, 14/10 h), but in wheat under HT, there was a decline in the degree of lipid unsaturation. PMC9183074.p007.s021: Several reports pronounced the alteration of lipid composition upon heat stress such as the increase in degree of saturated fatty acids or decrease in unsaturated fatty acids. PMC9183074.p007.s022: Hu et al. proposed that an increase in unsaturated fatty acids enhanced lipid thermostability in a photosynthetic membrane and might contribute to heat tolerance in various species.

events.event_label: MGDG and DGDG accumulate in thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s009: The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG). PMC9183074.p026.s010: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively. PMC9183074.p026.s011: The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability. PMC9183074.p026.s012: The major types of fatty acids that constitute lipids (MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG) in the plant thylakoid membrane are palmitic acid (C16:0), palmitoleic acid (C16:1), roughanic acid (C16:3), oleic acid (C18:1), linoleic acid (C18:2), and linolenic acid (C18:3). PMC9183074.p026.s013: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%.

events.event_label: Capric acid detected in Thai rice
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s006: A significant increase in long-chain fatty acids could be related to enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax and hence thicker cuticles in these cultivars. PMC9183074.p027.s007: Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s008: Although IR64 showed significant increases in the saturated long-chain fatty acids, its proposed enhanced cuticular wax synthesis might be offset by its weaker antioxidant systems as evidenced by high MDA content, leading to higher ROS-induced cellular damage. PMC9183074.p027.s009: Moreover, saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), under HT and control was found in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s010: In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2).

events.event_label: HT increases saturated fatty acids in thylakoid and cellular membranes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p006.s003: Thus, to decrease membrane fluidity under HT, lipid unsaturations could be decreased by downregulating the fatty acid desaturase (FAD) gene. PMC9183074.p006.s004: Maintaining the optimum composition of unsaturated fatty acids is important in determining cellular membrane fluidity under HT. PMC9183074.p006.s005: For heat-tolerant plants under HT, the degree of saturated fatty acids increases in both thylakoid membranes and cellular membranes. PMC9183074.p006.s006: This enhances lipid heat stability resulting in thermal tolerance in diverse species. PMC9183074.p006.s007: Some studies showed that changed lipid saturation was involved in the plant’s ability to tolerate heat.

events.event_label: HT reduces spikelet fertility by inhibiting anther dehiscence and decreasing pollination
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s003: Rice can be cultivated and grows well in a wide geographical and climatic range. PMC9183074.p007.s004: Elevated temperature can impose adverse effects on rice at different growth stages (such as germination, seedling, tillering, reproductive and grain filling stages) and induce reductions in rice yield. PMC9183074.p007.s005: At the flowering stage, HT reduces spikelet fertility by inhibiting anther dehiscence and hence decreasing pollination. PMC9183074.p007.s006: Additionally, at panicle initiation (PI), HT causes spikelet degeneration and damages the development of flower organs. PMC9183074.p007.s007: The reduction in spikelet fertility can lead to a severe reduction in rice yield.

events.event_label: HT stress impairs membrane stability
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s001: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A). PMC9183074.p012.s002: When subjected to HT stress, cellular membranes were damaged, and all rice genotypes showed significantly higher EL. PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22.

events.event_label: Unsaturated fatty acids localize to thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s018: In contrast, a decreased lipid saturation level in KDML105 led to lower membrane stability, hence more solute leakage (Figure 2), and dissociation of the photosystem and electron transport components, leading to a significant decline in PSII efficiency (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p026.s019: Total unsaturated fatty acids decreased in all rice cultivars under HT. PMC9183074.p026.s020: A lower content of unsaturated fatty acids, namely linolenic acid (C18:2), γ-linolenic acid (C18:3n6) and linolenic acid (C18:3), which are major components of the thylakoid membrane, helped maintain optimal fluidity and prevent the lipid peroxidation process, which predominantly attacks double bonds. PMC9183074.p026.s021: Moreover, the percentages of reduction in unsaturated fatty acids were greater than those in saturated fatty acids, resulting in an increased ratio between saturated:unsaturated fatty acids (Table 2 and Table 3 and Figure 4). PMC9183074.p026.s022: The highest percent increase in the ratio between saturated:unsaturated fatty acids in N22 could be related to its highest thermotolerance, as indicated by the unchanged Fv/Fm (Figure 1), lowest lipid peroxidation and EL (Figure 2).

events.event_label: unsaturated fatty acids detected in seedlings
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p019.s001: A total of nine unsaturated fatty acids were identified, and the contents under the control and HT treatment are shown in Table 2. PMC9183074.p019.s002: Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions. PMC9183074.p019.s004: In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.

events.event_label: HT induces lipid peroxidation causing membrane damage

events.event_label: HT alters fatty acid composition affecting thylakoid membrane stability
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p043.s001: HT caused differential alterations in fatty acid profiles, especially those comprising the thylakoid membrane in rice cultivars differing in heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p043.s002: In the case of N22 and CN1, their leaves slightly decreased the degree of lipid thylakoid membrane composition in the amount of palmitic acid (C16:0), linolenic acid (C18:2), γ-linolenic acid (C18:3n6) and linolenic acid (C18:3) and increased the amount of palmitoleic acid (C16:1). PMC9183074.p043.s003: The greater reduction in unsaturated fatty acids under HT, especially in N22, was related to maintenance of optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and increased membrane stability. PMC9183074.p043.s004: Moreover, in N22 and CN1, long-chain fatty acids, which serve as precursors of cuticular wax synthesis, namely arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0), lignoceric acid (24:0) and erucic acid (C22:1), also increased.

events.event_label: HT decreases unsaturated fatty acids and increases membrane stability

events.event_label: HT influences heat tolerance and electrolyte leakage
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s005: Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm. events.event_label: HT induces lipid peroxidation causing electrolyte leakage

events.event_label: Fatty acids detected in leaf and Thai rice
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s001: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress. PMC9183074.p027.s002: Our study analysed fatty acid compositions of the whole leaf; therefore, many fatty acids not previously reported in heat-stress studies were identified and displayed some interesting patterns of responses. PMC9183074.p027.s003: Among saturated fatty acids, arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0) (Table 1) sharply increased in N22, CN1 and IR64, while these acids significantly reduced in KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s004: In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2). PMC9183074.p027.s006: A significant increase in long-chain fatty acids could be related to enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax and hence thicker cuticles in these cultivars. PMC9183074.p027.s007: Thicker cuticles could help reduce excessive transpirational water loss during HT stress and increase light dispersion; therefore, alleviated cellular damage was evidenced by lower EL and higher Fv/Fm, particularly in N22 and CN1, compared to those of KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s008: Although IR64 showed significant increases in the saturated long-chain fatty acids, its proposed enhanced cuticular wax synthesis might be offset by its weaker antioxidant systems as evidenced by high MDA content, leading to higher ROS-induced cellular damage. PMC9183074.p027.s009: Moreover, saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), under HT and control was found in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s010: In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2).

events.event_label: HT impairs reproductive structures, reduces photosynthetic efficiency, and increases lipid peroxidation and electrolyte leakage
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s003: Rice can be cultivated and grows well in a wide geographical and climatic range. PMC9183074.p007.s012: Riceberry, and in rice cvs. PMC9183074.p007.s013: IR64 and KDML105 at the dough grain stage. evidence.text: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days.

relations.evidence_preview: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s001: Rice cultivated in the tropics is exposed to high temperature (HT) stress which threatens its growth and survival. PMC9183074.p002.s002: This study aimed at characterizing the HT response in terms of PSII efficiency and membrane stability, and to identify leaf fatty acid changes that may be associated with HT tolerance or sensitivity of rice genotypes. PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).

relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm). PMC9183074.p002.s005: Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm. PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions.

relations.evidence_preview: It also causes a loss in membrane integrity, aging in plant cells, inhibition of plant growth and cell death.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s001: High temperature (HT) is one of the main limiting factors in crop growth and development. PMC9183074.p004.s002: It leads to morphological, physiological and biochemical changes in crops and restricts crop survival and their productivity by negatively affecting the photosynthetic process. PMC9183074.p004.s003: It also causes a loss in membrane integrity, aging in plant cells, inhibition of plant growth and cell death. PMC9183074.p004.s004: A temperature greater than 40 °C affects many crops by causing thermal injury, inhibiting photosynthesis and changing protein structure and function such as protein unfolding, denaturation, aggregation and inactivation of enzyme reactions.

relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT.

relations.evidence_preview: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s002: The thylakoid membrane, where the photosynthetic light reaction occurs, is also highly thermal sensitive. PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT.

relations.subject: MGDG

evidence.text: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively.

relations.evidence_preview: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s009: The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG). PMC9183074.p026.s010: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively. PMC9183074.p026.s011: The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability. PMC9183074.p026.s012: The major types of fatty acids that constitute lipids (MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG) in the plant thylakoid membrane are palmitic acid (C16:0), palmitoleic acid (C16:1), roughanic acid (C16:3), oleic acid (C18:1), linoleic acid (C18:2), and linolenic acid (C18:3). PMC9183074.p026.s013: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%.

PMC9183074.p043.s004: Moreover, in N22 and CN1, long-chain fatty acids, which serve as precursors of cuticular wax synthesis, namely arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0), lignoceric acid (24:0) and erucic acid (C22:1), also increased. PMC9183074.p043.s005: These long-chain fatty acids may contribute to increased thickness of the cuticle, which helps prevent excessive water loss and reflects strong light, resulting in lower HT-induced damage. PMC9183074.p043.s006: Better maintenance of membrane thermostability and thicker cuticles in heat-tolerant genotypes lead to less electrolyte leakage, lower lipid peroxidation and stable Fv/Fm compared to the more sensitive genotypes. PMC9183074.p043.s007: Thus, this study suggested that there may be some relationships between the lipid composition of the thylakoid membrane and cuticle characteristics in a rice leaf, and their ability to tolerate HT.

sentences.text: High Temperature Alters Leaf Lipid Membrane Composition Associated with Photochemistry of PSII and Membrane Thermostability in Rice Seedlings

Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).
Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm.

N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex.

Thus, the reduction in unsaturated fatty acid composition of the thylakoid membrane and dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to high photosynthetic performance and an enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax which further provided additional protective roles for heat tolerance ability in rice.
It also causes a loss in membrane integrity, aging in plant cells, inhibition of plant growth and cell death.
The cellular membrane is the most sensitive site affected by environmental stimulation, including HT.

HT has been reported to be associated with disrupting cell membranes, breaking down cell compartmentalization, leading to solute leakage and affecting cellular functions that cause physiological injuries and leaf senescence.
The disruption of a cellular membrane by HT is related to the increase in membrane lipid peroxidation, which occurs when double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids are attacked by ROS.
The increase in membrane fluidity resulted from an HT-induced higher degree of lipid unsaturation.
Thus, to decrease membrane fluidity under HT, lipid unsaturations could be decreased by downregulating the fatty acid desaturase (FAD) gene.
Maintaining the optimum composition of unsaturated fatty acids is important in determining cellular membrane fluidity under HT.
For heat-tolerant plants under HT, the degree of saturated fatty acids increases in both thylakoid membranes and cellular membranes.
For example, roots of heat-tolerant creeping bentgrass exhibited increased lipid saturation under HT.
Avanza 641 responded to HT by decreasing the level of unsaturation in membrane lipid, especially in unsaturated linolenic acid (18:3) to maintain optimal membrane fluidity.
In contrast, some experiments demonstrated that higher degrees of unsaturated fatty acids can maintain membrane fluidity under HT.
For example, tall fescue genotypes that exhibited an increased degree of unsaturated fatty acids showed an improvement in heat tolerance.

Elevated temperature can impose adverse effects on rice at different growth stages (such as germination, seedling, tillering, reproductive and grain filling stages) and induce reductions in rice yield.
At the flowering stage, HT reduces spikelet fertility by inhibiting anther dehiscence and hence decreasing pollination.
A lower maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm) was found in tall fescue grass exposed to 35/30 °C (day/night) for 20 days, and in spinach stressed at 50 °C.
Moreover, HT also stimulated lipid peroxidation, resulting in a decline in cellular membrane thermal tolerance.

Previous reports in rice showed that HT led to significantly increased lipid peroxidation and electrolyte leakage, in cvs. Dular, IR64 and KDML105 at seedling, and in cvs. IR64 and KDML105 at the dough grain stage.
Several reports pronounced the alteration of lipid composition upon heat stress such as the increase in degree of saturated fatty acids or decrease in unsaturated fatty acids.
Hu et al. proposed that an increase in unsaturated fatty acids enhanced lipid thermostability in a photosynthetic membrane and might contribute to heat tolerance in various species.
Therefore, the experimental results inferring the relationship between lipid composition (degree of fatty acid saturation/unsaturation) and heat tolerance in various species are still inconclusive.

All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.
Total fatty acids in seedlings of all four rice cultivars after an exposure to HT sharply reduced by 1.
The highest total fatty acid content was found in CN1 for both the non-stress (10.
Only N22 and KDML105 showed slightly significant reductions in total saturated fatty acid contents after exposure to HT.
In contrast, unsaturated fatty acid contents in all rice cultivars were markedly reduced compared to the controls.
A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1.

Eight saturated fatty acids, namely lauric acid (C12:0), myristic acid (C14:0), pentadecanoic acid (C15:0), palmitic acid (C16:0), stearic acid (C18:0), arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0), were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars in both control and HT conditions.
Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.
Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments.
Palmitic acid and stearic acid markedly decreased with HT in all cultivars.

Three saturated fatty acids including arachidic acid, behenic acid, and lignoceric acid in N22, CN1 and IR64 were significantly increased after HT treatment, but in KDML105, the contents of these acids declined significantly.

Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions.
The content of palmitoleic acid in the HT plants of CN1 and KDML105 was present several folds higher than those in N22 and IR64.
Upon HT exposure, monounsaturated fatty acids significantly increased in N22 and IR64, but significantly decreased in Thai rice, CN1.
Sharp increases in Sat:Unsat after exposure to HT occurred in all rice cultivars in the range between 1.

HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis.
Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.

In this study, PSII efficiency was determined by measuring the maximum quantum yield in the dark-adapted stage (Fv/Fm), which decreased significantly after exposure to HT in CN1, KDML105 and IR64 but remained unchanged in the heat-tolerant genotype, N22 (Figure 1).

Heat-induced reduction in Fv/Fm resulted from an increase in minimum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (F0) which indicated the inhibition of electron transport and a reduction in maximum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (Fm) that is caused by a dissociation of peripheral antenna complex of PSII from its core complex.
This indicated that the ability to maintain PSII efficiency varies with developmental stage.
The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT.

HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death.
The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability.
Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%.

A lower content of unsaturated fatty acids, namely linolenic acid (C18:2), γ-linolenic acid (C18:3n6) and linolenic acid (C18:3), which are major components of the thylakoid membrane, helped maintain optimal fluidity and prevent the lipid peroxidation process, which predominantly attacks double bonds.

Moreover, the percentages of reduction in unsaturated fatty acids were greater than those in saturated fatty acids, resulting in an increased ratio between saturated:unsaturated fatty acids (Table 2 and Table 3 and Figure 4).

Similar to our results, HT treatment in Brassica carinata resulted in remodelling of leaf lipidome manifested by decreases in unsaturated levels of membrane lipids, especially C18:3, and increases in saturated fatty acids such as C16:0.

Our study analysed fatty acid compositions of the whole leaf; therefore, many fatty acids not previously reported in heat-stress studies were identified and displayed some interesting patterns of responses.
Moreover, saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), under HT and control was found in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.
HT caused differential alterations in fatty acid profiles, especially those comprising the thylakoid membrane in rice cultivars differing in heat tolerance.
The greater reduction in unsaturated fatty acids under HT, especially in N22, was related to maintenance of optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and increased membrane stability.

Moreover, in N22 and CN1, long-chain fatty acids, which serve as precursors of cuticular wax synthesis, namely arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0), lignoceric acid (24:0) and erucic acid (C22:1), also increased. These long-chain fatty acids may contribute to increased thickness of the cuticle, which helps prevent excessive water loss and reflects strong light, resulting in lower HT-induced damage.
